book_claims.mechanism: Rich in folate, iron, vitamin A, and vitamin E
book_claims.text: Featured vegetable across multiple Longevity Diet meals; top folate source (131 mcg per ½ cup boiled, 33% DV) and iron (3 mg per ½ cup boiled, 17% DV); also provides vitamin A (573 mcg RAE per ½ cup frozen/boiled, 229% DV) and vitamin E (1.9 mg per ½ cup boiled, 10% DV)

supplementary_research.finding: Spinach provides thylakoid membranes that suppress appetite hormones and improve satiety; also associated with reduced DNA oxidative damage
Spinach iron bioavailability is low (~1–2%) due to high oxalate content; cooking and vitamin C co-ingestion improves non-heme iron absorption

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: ~50% bioavailability; cooking increases concentration per gram but reduces total folate
Only ~2% absorbed due to oxalate; pair with vitamin C source to improve absorption
Conversion from beta-carotene; fat required for absorption

synergies.note: Vitamin C from lemon enhances non-heme iron absorption from spinach, partially offsetting oxalate inhibition
Fat essential for absorption of fat-soluble vitamins A, E, and K concentrated in spinach
Traditional Catalan/Sicilian spinach con pasas; raisins add sweetness and iron alongside spinach in Longevity Diet recipe
